1: A shadow: Qohelet, the Assembler/Preacher, speaks. The title qohelet derives from qahal (assembly, congregation) — the one who gathers or addresses the assembly. Christ is the ultimate Preacher-Teacher who gathers his assembly (Matthew 5:1: he opened his mouth and taught; Luke 4:18-19: the Spirit of the Lord is upon me to preach). But where Qohelet gathers to deliver a verdict of vanity, Christ gathers to announce the kingdom — not 'all is hebel' but 'blessed are you.' The book's opening word signals the whole: a wisdom preacher addressing God's people about the nature of human existence.
2: A type: 'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity.' The Hebrew hevel (breath, vapor) is the diagnosis of the post-fall condition of creation — everything human striving builds dissolves like breath in cold air. Paul identifies this directly in Romans 8:20: 'the creation was subjected to futility [mataiotes, the LXX word for hevel] not willingly, but because of him who subjected it, in hope.' The hevel-condition is the consequence of the fall; Christ's work is the answer. The 'not in vain' of 1 Corinthians 15:58 — 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain' — is the direct reversal of 'all is vanity': the resurrection of Christ breaks the hevel-cycle. Qohelet's diagnosis is accurate; the cure is what he cannot yet see.
3: A theme: 'What does man gain [yitron] from all his toil at which he toils under the sun?' Yitron is a commercial term — surplus, profit left over after expenses. Qohelet's question is: what is the net gain of a human life? His answer will be 'nothing.' Paul answers from the other side of the resurrection: 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Corinthians 15:58); 'to live is Christ, and to die is gain' (Philippians 1:21). The yitron-question Qohelet asks, Christ answers by being himself the profit: in him, labor has eternal weight. The 'under the sun' qualifier matters — all toil within the merely earthly frame produces no lasting surplus. Christ breaks the frame.
4: A shadow: 'A generation goes, and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever.' The endless cycle of human generations without permanence — what one generation builds, the next inherits and then loses. Hebrews 1:10-12 addresses this directly, quoting Psalm 102 of Christ: 'You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands; they will perish, but you remain; they will all wear out like a garment... but you are the same, and your years will have no end.' The creation that 'remains forever' by Qohelet's observation does not actually remain — even it will perish. Only Christ remains unchanged through all the generational cycling.
5: A shadow: 'The sun rises, and the sun sets, and hastens to its place where it rises.' The sun's endless cycle — the image of relentless, purposeless repetition. John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' The sun Qohelet observes cycling without purpose becomes in Christ the image of the true light who does not cycle into darkness: Revelation 22:5 — 'night will be no more. They will need no light of lamp or sun, for the Lord God will be their light.' The solar cycle is the creation-image for hevel; Christ is the light that ends the cycle.
6: A shadow: 'The wind goes toward the south and turns to the north; around and around goes the wind, and on its circuits the wind returns.' The wind's purposeless circling reinforces the vanity-of-cycles motif. But Christ uses the wind as the image of the Spirit's sovereign, purposive work: 'The wind blows where it wishes, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes. So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit' (John 3:8). The wind that Qohelet observes cycling in meaningless circuits is reframed by Christ as the image of the Spirit's free and regenerating movement — the same wind, but now purposive and life-giving rather than vanity-illustrating.
7: A shadow: 'All streams run to the sea, yet the sea is not full; to the place where the streams flow, there they flow again.' The unfillable sea — the image of unsatisfied longing, the human condition of desire that nothing satisfies. Revelation 22:1 presents the eschatological fulfillment: 'Then the angel showed me the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb.' The sea that is never full in Qohelet's observation becomes in the new creation the river of life that flows from the Lamb's throne — not an endless unsatisfied cycle, but a perpetual outflow of life. John 4:14: 'the water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life.'
8: A direct connection: 'All things are full of weariness; a man cannot utter it; the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.' The unsatisfied eye and ear — no earthly thing can fill the human capacity for seeing and hearing. Christ's answer is direct: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst' (John 6:35). The eye not satisfied with seeing is satisfied when it sees Christ (John 14:9: 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father'). The ear not filled with hearing is filled by his words (John 6:68: 'Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life'). What no earthly thing could fill, Christ fills.
9: A type: 'What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun.' The no-novelty principle — history as endless recurrence with no new thing. 2 Corinthians 5:17: 'Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come.' Christ is the absolute exception to the nothing-new principle: his resurrection is the first genuinely new thing since the creation — a body that has passed through death and emerged transformed, not merely resuscitated. The new creation is not a repetition of the old. Revelation 21:5: 'Behold, I am making all things new.' The thing Qohelet declares impossible — something genuinely new — is precisely what Christ brings.
10: A shadow: 'Is there a thing of which it is said, See, this is new? It has been already in the ages before us.' The skeptic's challenge to novelty claims — everything that seems new has already existed before. Christ's response to this entire trajectory is the resurrection, which is categorically unprecedented: no one in history had ever been raised with a glorified, deathless body. 1 Corinthians 15:20: 'But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' The firstfruits language signals absolute novelty: a first-of-its-kind event. The 'already been before us' claim is the very thing the resurrection refutes.
11: A shadow: 'There is no remembrance of former things, nor will there be any remembrance of things that are to come among those who come after.' The oblivion of human memory — nothing is permanently remembered, everything fades. Isaiah 43:18-19: 'Remember not the former things, nor consider the things of old. Behold, I am doing a new thing; now it springs forth, do you not perceive it?' And Revelation 21:4: 'He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away.' In the new creation, what passes away is not the memory of the righteous (Psalms 112:6: 'the righteous will be remembered forever') but the former sufferings — a reversal of Qohelet's amnesia principle.
12: A shadow: 'I the Preacher have been king over Israel in Jerusalem.' Qohelet identifies himself as the royal wisdom-seeker. Christ is the ultimate royal wisdom-seeker — not an earthly king exploring wisdom, but Wisdom himself made flesh and becoming the king who has 'searched out by wisdom all that is done under heaven' (Ecclesiastes 1:13), and whose verdict is not vanity but 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life' (John 14:6). The king-wisdom figure that Solomon represents is a type of Christ the king-Wisdom (1 Corinthians 1:24: 'Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God').
13: A theme: 'And I applied my heart to seek and to search out by wisdom all that is done under heaven. It is an unhappy business that God has given to the children of man to be busy with.' The wisdom-quest applied to all human activity — Qohelet's comprehensive investigation. Colossians 2:3: 'In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.' What Qohelet sought by investigation, Christ is by nature: the one in whom all wisdom is hidden. The 'unhappy business' of wisdom-seeking under the sun becomes in Christ the joyful discovery that wisdom has come down and made itself accessible. John 14:6: 'I am the truth' — the search is over.
14: A theme: 'I have seen everything that is done under the sun, and behold, all is vanity and a striving after wind.' The conclusion of the wisdom-survey: all earthly achievement and knowledge produces only vanity. John 6:27: 'Do not work for the food that perishes, but for the food that endures to eternal life, which the Son of Man will give to you.' The labor that produces only vanity (striving after wind) is contrasted with labor directed toward Christ, who gives the food that endures. The shift from 'all is vanity' to 'labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Corinthians 15:58) depends entirely on whether the labor is 'under the sun' (earthly frame only) or 'in the Lord' (resurrection frame).
15: A type pointing to prophetic fulfillment: 'What is crooked cannot be made straight, and what is lacking cannot be counted.' Qohelet's statement of the limits of human wisdom — there are disorders that wisdom cannot correct. Isaiah 40:4 and Luke 3:5 announce the opposite: 'every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be made low, and the crooked shall become straight, and the rough places shall become level ways.' The impossible that Qohelet declares — making crooked things straight — is precisely what John the Baptist announces as the work of Christ's coming. The theological claim of the gospel is that what human wisdom cannot accomplish, God's intervention through Christ can and will.
16: A shadow: 'I said in my heart, 'I have acquired great wisdom, surpassing all who were before me over Jerusalem, and my heart has had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.'' Solomon's claim to surpassing wisdom — the zenith of human wisdom achievement. Matthew 12:42: 'The queen of the South came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.' Christ's claim to be greater than Solomon — the very pinnacle Qohelet invokes — positions him as the one who stands beyond the entire wisdom-competition frame. What Solomon achieved was the maximum of human wisdom; Christ is divine wisdom in person (1 Corinthians 1:24).
17: A shadow: 'And I applied my heart to know wisdom and to know madness and folly. I perceived that this also is but a striving after wind.' Even the pursuit of wisdom itself turns out to be vanity — the ultimate irony of the wisdom-quest. 1 Corinthians 3:19: 'the wisdom of this world is folly with God.' Paul takes the Qohelet diagnosis and applies it theologically: earthly wisdom that does not reckon with God is foolishness. The inversion of Christ's cross becomes the new epistemology: 'the foolishness of God is wiser than men' (1 Corinthians 1:25). The wisdom Qohelet found to be striving after wind is precisely what Paul identifies as the world's pseudo-wisdom; the real wisdom is the crucified Christ.
18: A shadow: 'For in much wisdom is much vexation, and he who increases knowledge increases sorrow.' The wisdom-grief formula — the more Qohelet knows, the more he suffers from knowing. Isaiah 53:3: 'He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief.' Christ, the fullness of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3), is also the man of sorrows — the one who, knowing everything, bore the maximum of grief. The Qohelet formula points to the incarnation: to enter fully into human wisdom is to enter fully into human grief, which is exactly what the Son of God did. The wisdom-sorrow that Qohelet observes is fulfilled in the one who is both the fullness of wisdom and the bearer of human sorrow.
